title: आपः सूक्तम्
devanagariText:
॥ श्री आपः सूक्तम् ॥

ॐ आपो हि ष्ठा मयोभुवस्ता न ऊर्जे दधातन।
महे रणाय चक्षसे॥ १॥

यो वः शिवतमो रसस्तस्य भाजयतेह नः।
उशतीरिव मातरः॥ २॥

तस्मा अरं गमाम वो यस्य क्षयाय जिन्वथ।
आपो जनयथा च नः॥ ३॥

शं नो देवीरभिष्टय आपो भवन्तु पीतये।
शं योरभि स्रवन्तु नः॥ ४॥

ईशाना वार्याणां क्षयन्तीश्चर्षणीनाम्।
अपो याचामि भेषजम्॥ ५॥

अप्सु मे सोमो अब्रवीदन्तर्विश्वानि भेषजा।
अग्निं च विश्वशम्भुवम्॥ ६॥

आपः पृणीत भेषजं वरूथं तन्वे मम।
ज्योक्च सूर्यं दृशे॥ ७॥

इदमापः प्र वहत यत्किं च दुरितं मयि।
यद्वाहमभिदुद्रोह यद्वा शेप उतानृतम्॥ ८॥

आपो अद्यान्वचारिषं रसेन समगस्महि।
पयस्वानग्न आ गहि तं मा सं सृज वर्चसा॥ ९॥

हिरण्यवर्णाः शुचयः पावकाः यासु जातः कश्यपो यासु अग्निः।
यासु इन्द्रो वरुणो या अमृतं यासु ताऽआपो देवीरिह मामवन्तु॥ १०॥

ॐ शान्तिः शान्तिः शान्तिः॥
transliteration:
|| Shri Apah Suktam ||

Om Apo hi shtha mayo-bhuvas-ta na urje dadhatana |
Mahe ranaya chakshase || 1 ||

Yo vah shivatamo rasas-tasya bhajayateha nah |
Ushatir-iva matarah || 2 ||

Tasma aram gamama vo yasya kshayaya jinvatha |
Apo janayatha cha nah || 3 ||

Sham no devir-abhishtaya apo bhavantu pitaye |
Sham yor-abhi sravantu nah || 4 ||

Ishana varyaanam kshayantish-charshinam |
Apo yachami bheshajam || 5 ||

Apsu me Somo abravid-antar-vishvani bheshaja |
Agnim cha vishva-shambhuvam || 6 ||

Apah prinita bheshajam varutham tanve mama |
Jyok-cha Suryam drishe || 7 ||

Idam-apah pra vahata yat-kim-cha duritam mayi |
Yad-vaham-abhi-dudroha yad-va shepa utanritam || 8 ||

Apo adyanv-acharisham rasena samagasmahi |
Payasvan-Agna aa gahi tam ma sam srija varchasaa || 9 ||

Hiranya-varnah shuchayah pavakah yasu jatah Kashyapo yasu Agnih |
Yasu Indro Varuno ya amritam yasu ta apo devir-iha mam-avantu || 10 ||

Om Shantih Shantih Shantih ||
hindiMeaning:
जल सचमुच सुखदायक हैं — वे हमें ऊर्जा प्रदान करें, महान आनन्द और दृष्टि के लिए॥ १॥

आपका जो सर्वाधिक शिवकर (कल्याणकारी) रस है, उसमें हमें भागी बनाइए — जैसे स्नेही माताएँ (सन्तान को)॥ २॥

हम उस रस की ओर सहर्ष चलें जिसके निवास हेतु आप प्रेरित करती हैं। हे जल! हमें जन्म (पुनर्जीवन) भी दो॥ ३॥

दिव्य जल हमारे पीने के लिए शुभ हों, सुखप्रद हों। कल्याण और आनन्द हमारी ओर प्रवाहित हों॥ ४॥

वरणीय पदार्थों की स्वामिनी, प्रजाओं पर शासन करने वाली — जल से मैं औषधि माँगता हूँ॥ ५॥

जल में सोम ने मुझसे कहा कि जल में समस्त औषधियाँ हैं और विश्वकल्याणकारी अग्नि भी है॥ ६॥

हे जल! मेरे शरीर के लिए औषधि और रक्षा प्रदान करो, और चिरकाल तक सूर्य देखने के लिए (दीर्घायु)॥ ७॥

हे जल! मुझमें जो भी दुरित (पाप) है, जो मैंने द्रोह किया या शाप दिया या असत्य बोला — वह सब बहा ले जाओ॥ ८॥

आज मैं जल का सेवन किया, रस से हम एक हुए। हे पयस्वान अग्नि! आओ, मुझे तेज से समृद्ध करो॥ ९॥

हिरण्यवर्ण (स्वर्ण-रंगी), शुद्ध, पवित्र जल — जिनमें कश्यप जन्मे, जिनमें अग्नि, जिनमें इन्द्र-वरुण, जिनमें अमृत — वे दिव्य जल यहाँ मेरी रक्षा करें॥ १०॥
benefits: Sacred hymn to divine waters — the source of all life
Powerful purification mantra for body, mind, and soul
Waters contain all medicines (Vishvani Bheshaja) — promotes healing
Washes away sins, guilt, and negative karma
Essential chanting during Sandhya Vandana and Vedic rituals
Ideal for water purification ceremonies and Jala Abhishekam
description: Dedicated to Lord Vishnu, the Apah Suktam (आपः सूक्तम्) is a Vedic hymn revered in Hindu tradition. Attributed to Rigveda (Mandala 10, Sukta 9), this prayer holds a special place in Hindu devotional literature. Regular recitation is believed to water contain all medicines (Vishvani Bheshaja) — promotes healing, wash away sins, guilt, and negative karma, and grant longevity, vitality, and radiance (Varchas). This prayer is traditionally recited on Monday (Somvar) and Wednesday (Budhvar) to invoke divine grace. At 10 verses long, the recitation takes roughly 6 minutes, making it accessible for regular devotional use. On this page, you can read the complete Apah Suktam in Devanagari Sanskrit with English transliteration, Hindi meaning (arth), and free PDF download for offline recitation.
seoDescription: Read Apah Suktam in Sanskrit with transliteration and Hindi meaning. Rigvedic water hymn for purification, healing, removal of sins, and divine blessings.

seoFaqs.question: What is the Apah Suktam and where is it preserved in the Veda?
seoFaqs.answer: The Apah Suktam (आपः सूक्तम्) — literally 'the hymn of the waters' — is a foundational Vedic hymn addressed to the divine waters (apah) personified as goddesses (apo devih) who are the source of all life, healing, purification and immortality. The principal text is preserved in the Rigveda — Mandala 10, Sukta 9 (mantras 1-9 'Apo hi shtha mayobhuvah') by the rishi Sindhudvipa Ambarisha or Trishiras Tvashtra, with closely-related apah hymns also at Rigveda 7.49, Atharvaveda 1.5-6, and the well-known concluding mantra 'Hiranyavarnah shuchayah pavakah' from the Taittiriya Aranyaka. The Apah Suktam is among the most ancient and most-recited Vedic prayers in Hindu tradition, forming a core component of every Sandhya-vandana, every Sankalpa, every Achamana, every Snana-vidhi and every Kalasha-puja across the entire Hindu world. (Sources: Rigveda 10.9; Taittiriya Aranyaka; classical Vedic recitation tradition.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the meaning of the famous opening Apo hi shtha mayobhuvah?
seoFaqs.answer: The opening mantra — Apo hi shtha mayobhuvas-ta na urje dadhatana, mahe ranaya chakshase — is one of the most universally chanted mantras of the Vedic tradition, used in every Sandhya-vandana and many Vedic rituals. Word-by-word: Apah — O waters; hi shtha — verily you are; mayobhuvah — bringers of bliss, generators of joy; ta nah — therefore please for us; urje dadhatana — bestow vigour, vital energy (urja); mahe — for great; ranaya — joy, delight; chakshase — for vision, for insight. The full meaning: 'O waters! You are the very source of bliss; please therefore bestow on us vital energy (urja) for the supreme delight (rana) of insight (chakshu).' The verse worships water not as a mere physical element but as a deva-shakti, a goddess-power that brings life, joy, health and clarity. (Sources: Rigveda 10.9.1; classical Vedic commentary tradition.)

seoFaqs.question: Why are the waters (apah) addressed as goddesses (devih) and mothers (matarah) in the Veda?
seoFaqs.answer: The Vedic seers experienced the waters as living, conscious deva-shakti — the divine feminine in her purifying, nurturing and life-giving form. The Apah Suktam addresses them in three principal ways: (1) Apo Devih — the divine goddesses of water (mantra 4 'Sham no devir abhishtaya apo bhavantu pitaye'); (2) Matarah — mothers (mantra 2 'Yo vah shivatamo rasas tasya bhajayateha nah, ushatir-iva matarah' — 'Of your most auspicious essence please share with us as loving mothers share with their children'); (3) Ishana varyanam — sovereigns of all desirables (mantra 5). The deeper Vedic insight is that water is the original womb of life — every being is born in amniotic water, the earth's life springs from rivers and oceans, plants drink water to live, and even the cosmos in many Vedic accounts begins with the apah from which Hiranyagarbha emerges. To honour water is to honour the supreme nurturing aspect of the divine. (Sources: Rigveda 10.9.2-5; classical Vedic theology.)

seoFaqs.question: How is the Apah Suktam used in Sandhya Vandana and daily ritual?
seoFaqs.answer: The Apah Suktam is woven through every part of the brahmanical sandhya-vandana ritual, performed at the three sandhyas (dawn, noon and dusk) by initiated dvija. The principal uses: (1) Achamana — sipping water purification before any ritual, accompanied by 'Apo hi shtha mayobhuvah' and the names of Vishnu; (2) Marjana — sprinkling water over oneself to purify body and aura, with the full Apah Suktam mantras 1-9 chanted while taking a few drops of water on the kushasana grass and sprinkling head, eyes, nose, mouth, ears, shoulders and chest; (3) Aghamarshana — the 'sin-carrying-away' mantra (Apah Suktam mantra 8 'Idam apah pra vahata yat kim cha duritam mayi') in which water is held in the cupped palm, sins are mentally placed in it and the water is then poured out; (4) Arghya — offering of water to Surya at sunrise and sunset; (5) Kalasha-sthapana for any puja, where the Apah Suktam consecrates the kalasha water as Mata Apo Devi. (Sources: classical Sandhya Vandana paddhati.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the Aghamarshana mantra and how is it used?
seoFaqs.answer: The Aghamarshana mantra — Apah Suktam mantra 8 — is one of the most profound purification practices of the Vedic tradition. The full mantra reads: 'Idam apah pra vahata yat kim cha duritam mayi, yad vaham abhi-dudroha yad va shepa utanritam' — 'O waters, please carry away (pra-vahata) whatever sin (durita) is in me, whatever harm I have done (abhi-dudroha), whatever curses I have spoken (shepa), and whatever falsehood I have uttered (anrita).' The vidhi: take a kushasana, hold a few drops of water in the cupped right palm (kushodaka), close the eyes, mentally place all sins of body, speech and mind into the water, recite the mantra with intent, then pour the water out at the base of a tulsi or onto the bare earth (never onto a paved surface). The Vedic understanding is that water — the universal solvent and original purifier — has the spiritual capacity to literally carry sin away when invoked as devi with sankalpa. (Sources: Rigveda 10.9.8; classical Aghamarshana paddhati.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the meaning of Apsu antar amritam apsu bheshajam in the suktam?
seoFaqs.answer: This phrase — appearing across multiple mantras of the Apah Suktam (mantras 6-7 'Apsu me Somo abravid antar vishvani bheshaja, Agnim cha vishva-shambhuvam... Apah prinita bheshajam') — declares one of the most striking truths of the Vedic medical tradition. Word-by-word: Apsu — in the waters; antar — within, inside; amritam — the immortal essence (amrita, the nectar of immortality); apsu bheshajam — in the waters (lies) the medicine; vishvani bheshaja — all medicines (are within the waters); vishva-shambhuvam Agnim — and the all-blessing Agni too. The verse expresses the Vedic-Ayurvedic conviction that water is the universal carrier of healing — every herb's medicinal essence is extracted in water (decoction, infusion); soma the divine nectar is found in waters; even the inner Agni (digestive fire, life-fire) is sustained by water. Tirtha-snana, jala-chikitsa, abhisheka, and gangajala drinking all derive their authority from this Vedic teaching. (Sources: Rigveda 10.9.6-7; Ayurvedic tradition.)
